results.possibleIdentity: David W. McCullough (born 1945) is an American interdisciplinary artist known for his work in painting, sculpture, and performance art, often incorporating elements of science and technology. Born in Springfield, Massachusetts, he studied art and technology at Springfield Technical High School (1963–1965) and later earned a B.F.A. in painting and printmaking from the Kansas City Art Institute (1968–1970). During the late 1960s, he participated in avant-garde 'happenings' with Allan Kaprow and Dick Higgins. McCullough moved to Dallas, Texas, in 1970, where he became a prominent figure in the local art scene, executing projects like the 'Baggie Mantra Sanctorum March' (1971), considered Dallas's first outdoor environmental happening. His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is evidenced by his inclusion in archives documenting experimental art and technology collaborations from the late 1960s and 1970s, such as the Jack Eyerly collection. McCullough's practice, which he terms 'Quantum Art' or 'Sonic Expressionism,' often involves electronic music, sand paintings, and the use of synthesizers (including studies on the Moog synthesizer at the University of North Texas). He holds an M.F.A. from the California Institute of the Arts (CalArts) and a second M.F.A. in Art and Technology from the University of Texas at Dallas.

results.possibleIdentity: Lucio Pozzi (born November 29, 1935) is an Italian-born American multidisciplinary artist known for his work in painting, performance, installation, video, and sculpture. Born in Milan, he studied architecture in Rome before moving to New York City in 1962. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he became a prominent figure in the New York avant-garde scene. He was notably associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), appearing in their records for 'matchings' between artists and engineers from 1966 to 1968. Pozzi's work is characterized by a rejection of stylistic consistency, often alternating between minimalism and maximalism and utilizing 'translation mechanisms' like dualisms (e.g., the Level Group and A/Z diptych series). He founded the artist-run magazine New Observations in the mid-1970s and has held teaching positions at institutions such as Cooper Union, Yale University, Princeton University, and the School of Visual Arts. His work has been featured in major international exhibitions, including Documenta 6 (1977), the Venice Biennale (1980), and a solo video exhibition in the Museum of Modern Art's Projects series (1978).
